chunks.heading: New Tax Regime Slabs (AY 2025-26 / FY 2024-25)
chunks.content:
For Assessment Year (AY) 2025-26, corresponding to Financial Year (FY) 2024-25, the New Tax Regime is the default regime under Section 115BAC. The tax slabs are:
- Income up to ₹3,00,000: Nil (0%)
- Income from ₹3,00,001 to ₹7,00,000: 5%
- Income from ₹7,00,001 to ₹10,00,000: 10%
- Income from ₹10,00,001 to ₹12,00,000: 15%
- Income from ₹12,00,001 to ₹15,00,000: 20%
- Income above ₹15,00,000: 30%

Standard Deduction for salaried employees under the New Regime is ₹75,000 (increased from ₹50,000 by Finance Act 2024).

chunks.heading: Old Tax Regime Slabs (AY 2025-26 / FY 2024-25)
chunks.content:
Under the Old Tax Regime for AY 2025-26, the tax slabs for individuals below 60 years of age are:
- Income up to ₹2,50,000: Nil (0%)
- Income from ₹2,50,001 to ₹5,00,000: 5%
- Income from ₹5,00,001 to ₹10,00,000: 20%
- Income above ₹10,00,000: 30%

Standard Deduction for salaried employees under the Old Regime is ₹50,000. Under the Old Regime, deductions under Chapter VI-A (80C, 80D, etc.) and exemptions (HRA, LTA) are allowed.

chunks.heading: Tax Rebate under Section 87A
chunks.content:
Section 87A provides a tax rebate for resident individuals:
- Under the New Tax Regime, a rebate up to ₹20,000 is allowed if the total taxable income does not exceed ₹7,00,000. Marginal relief is available if the taxable income slightly exceeds ₹7,00,000.
- Under the Old Tax Regime, a rebate up to ₹12,500 is allowed if the total taxable income does not exceed ₹5,00,000.

chunks.heading: Section 80C - Deductions for Savings & Investments
chunks.content:
Section 80C of the Income Tax Act allows a deduction of up to ₹1,50,000 per financial year for investments in specified instruments. Eligible investments include:
- Public Provident Fund (PPF)
- Employee Provident Fund (EPF)
- Equity Linked Savings Scheme (ELSS) mutual funds
- National Savings Certificate (NSC)
- Life Insurance Premium payments
- Principal repayment of Home Loan
- Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana (SSY)
- Senior Citizens Savings Scheme (SCSS)
- Tuition/school fees for up to two children
Note: This deduction is only available under the Old Tax Regime. It is not allowed under the New Tax Regime.

chunks.heading: Section 80D - Medical Insurance Deduction
chunks.content:
Section 80D allows a deduction for health insurance premiums paid:
- Self, spouse, and dependent children: Up to ₹25,000 (increases to ₹50,000 if self/spouse is a senior citizen).
- Parents: Additional deduction up to ₹25,000 (increases to ₹50,000 if parents are senior citizens).
- Preventive health check-up: Up to ₹5,000 within the overall limit.
Note: This deduction is only available under the Old Tax Regime. It is not allowed under the New Tax Regime.

chunks.heading: Section 24(b) - Home Loan Interest Deduction
chunks.content:
Section 24(b) allows deduction of interest paid on a home loan:
- Self-occupied property: Up to ₹2,00,000 per year can be claimed as a deduction.
- Let-out property: No upper limit for interest deduction, but loss from house property that can be set off against other heads of income is capped at ₹2,00,000.
Note: Deduction for self-occupied home loan interest under Section 24(b) is only available under the Old Tax Regime. It is not allowed under the default New Tax Regime.

chunks.heading: Section 115BAC House Property Loss Set-off and Carry Forward Rules
chunks.content: Policy Guardrail: When evaluating the New Tax Regime (Section 115BAC), you must strictly enforce that any loss under the head 'Income from House Property' can neither be set off against any other head of income in the current year NOR carried forward to any future years. The loss lapses entirely. Never advise a user that they can carry forward a house property loss under the New Regime.

chunks.heading: House Rent Allowance (HRA) Exemption under Section 10(13A)
chunks.content:
House Rent Allowance (HRA) received by salaried employees is exempt under Section 10(13A) to the extent of the minimum of the following three:
1. Actual HRA received
2. Rent paid minus 10% of basic salary
3. 50% of basic salary (for metro cities: Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata, Chennai) or 40% (for non-metros)
Note: HRA exemption is only available under the Old Tax Regime. It is not allowed under the default New Tax Regime.

chunks.heading: Section 44AD - Presumptive Taxation for Businesses
chunks.content:
Section 44AD offers a presumptive taxation scheme for resident individuals, HUFs, and partnership firms (excluding LLPs) engaged in business:
- Eligibility: Turnover/gross receipts up to ₹2 Crores (increased to ₹3 Crores by Finance Act 2023/2024 if cash receipts are ≤ 5% of total turnover).
- Deemed profit rate: 8% of total turnover, or 6% of turnover received through digital/bank modes.
- Benefits: No requirement to maintain books of accounts (under Section 44AA) or get accounts audited (under Section 44AB).

chunks.heading: Section 44ADA - Presumptive Taxation for Professionals
chunks.content:
Section 44ADA offers a presumptive taxation scheme for specified professionals (doctors, lawyers, engineers, chartered accountants, architects, etc.):
- Eligibility: Gross receipts up to ₹50 Lakhs (increased to ₹75 Lakhs by Finance Act 2023/2024 if cash receipts are ≤ 5% of total gross receipts).
- Deemed profit rate: 50% of the total gross receipts is considered as taxable income.
- Benefits: No requirement to maintain books of accounts under Section 44AA or get accounts audited under Section 44AB.

chunks.heading: Short-Term Capital Gains (STCG) on Equity & Debt (AY 2025-26)
chunks.content:
For transactions on or after July 23, 2024 (Finance Act 2024):
- STCG under Section 111A on listed equity shares, equity-oriented mutual funds, and business trusts subject to Securities Transaction Tax (STT) is taxed at 20% (increased from 15%).
- STCG on debt-oriented mutual funds, market-linked debentures, and other non-equity assets is taxed at the individual's applicable slab rates (as these are classified as short-term capital gains regardless of holding period).

chunks.heading: Long-Term Capital Gains (LTCG) on Equity & Debt (AY 2025-26)
chunks.content:
For transactions on or after July 23, 2024 (Finance Act 2024):
- LTCG under Section 112A on listed equity shares, equity-oriented mutual funds, and business trusts is taxed at 12.5% (increased from 10%). The exemption limit for LTCG on these assets is increased to ₹1,25,000 (from ₹1,00,000) per financial year.
- LTCG on unlisted assets (e.g., real estate, unlisted shares) is taxed at 12.5% without indexation benefit (previously 20% with indexation).

chunks.heading: Key TDS Rates and Sections
chunks.content:
Common TDS sections under the Income Tax Act:
- Section 192: TDS on salary (computed on estimated income using applicable slab rates).
- Section 194C: TDS on payments to contractors (1% for individuals/HUFs, 2% for others).
- Section 194J: TDS on professional/technical services (10% standard rate, 2% for royalty or technical services, 2% for call center operators).
- Section 194I: TDS on rent (10% for land/building/furniture, 2% for machinery/plant/equipment; exemption limit is ₹2,40,000 per year).
- Section 194DA: TDS on life insurance payouts (5% on taxable income portion if payout is not exempt under Section 10(10D)).
